title: Extract duplicated badge color logic into Jinja2 macros
analysis:
Badge color logic is duplicated ~20 times across 4 partial templates. Each uses long if/elif chains mapping status/severity strings to Tailwind bg-color classes. Five semantic badge domains exist: task status (tasks.html — 5 branches, NO aria-labels), orchestrator status (orchestrators.html — boolean-derived running/completed, WITH aria-labels), burst state (recon.html — bursting/idle, WITH aria-labels), verdict severity (recon.html — 5 branches, WITH aria-labels), run status (recon.html — 6 branches, WITH aria-labels). Memory.html has 3 duplicated indicator dots (binary connected/disconnected) plus 1 tri-state queue dot.

Semantic conflict: 'running' maps to bg-green-600 in orchestrators (meaning 'alive/healthy') but bg-blue-600 in recon runs (meaning 'in progress'). Resolved via optional color override parameter on status_badge.

NOT extracted: (a) Write Queue 3-state health dot — 3 colors + 3 distinct aria-labels, doesn't fit boolean indicator_dot. (b) Progress bar div segments in orchestrators.html — structural HTML differs (div + width style). (c) Count badge pill in orchestrators.html — different styling.

Existing test coverage: 42+ integration tests across test_orchestrator_panel.py, test_recon_panel.py, test_memory_partial.py validate badge colors, aria-labels, and text. These serve as the regression safety net. Zero modifications to existing tests required.

Jinja2Templates directory is configured as _pkg_dir/'templates' (app.py line 28), so '{% from "macros/badges.html" import ... %}' resolves automatically with no app.py changes.

prerequisites.description: Create the macros/ directory under templates/ (mkdir -p dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/macros)

steps.description: Write failing unit tests for status_badge macro in dashboard/tests/test_badge_macros.py. Create a jinja2_env fixture that imports dashboard.app.templates.env. Test class TestStatusBadge renders via env.from_string('{% from "macros/badges.html" import status_badge %}{{ status_badge(...) }}'). Tests: (1) test_done_green — 'done' produces bg-green-600; (2) test_in_progress_blue — 'in-progress' produces bg-blue-600; (3) test_pending_yellow — 'pending' produces bg-yellow-600; (4) test_blocked_red — 'blocked' produces bg-red-600; (5) test_running_blue — 'running' produces bg-blue-600; (6) test_completed_green — 'completed' produces bg-green-600; (7) test_failed_red — 'failed' produces bg-red-600; (8) test_bursting_orange — 'bursting' produces bg-orange-600; (9) test_circuit_breaker_purple — 'circuit_breaker' produces bg-purple-600; (10) test_unknown_fallback_gray — 'xyz' produces bg-gray-600; (11) test_renders_status_text — badge contains status as visible text; (12) test_no_aria_label_by_default — aria_label not in output when called as status_badge(status); (13) test_aria_label_with_prefix — status_badge('done', aria_label='Run status') produces aria-label="Run status: done"; (14) test_color_override — status_badge('running', color='bg-green-600') uses green not blue; (15) test_badge_span_classes — output contains 'inline-block px-2 py-0.5 text-xs font-medium rounded' and 'text-white'.

Create badges.html in macros/ with status_badge(status, aria_label=None, color=None) macro. Color map dict: done/completed->bg-green-600, in-progress/running->bg-blue-600, pending->bg-yellow-600, blocked/failed->bg-red-600, cancelled/deferred/idle->bg-gray-600, bursting/rolled_back->bg-orange-600, circuit_breaker->bg-purple-600. Uses 'color if color else colors.get(status, "bg-gray-600")' for the bg class. Conditionally renders aria-label only if aria_label is not None: {% if aria_label %}aria-label="{{ aria_label }}: {{ status }}"{% endif %}. Renders <span> with standard badge classes.

Write failing unit tests for severity_badge macro in test_badge_macros.py. TestSeverityBadge class: (1) test_ok_green — 'ok' produces bg-green-600; (2) test_minor_yellow — 'minor' produces bg-yellow-600; (3) test_moderate_orange — 'moderate' produces bg-orange-600; (4) test_serious_red — 'serious' produces bg-red-600; (5) test_unknown_fallback_gray — 'xyz' produces bg-gray-600; (6) test_renders_severity_text — output contains severity string; (7) test_no_aria_label_by_default — no aria-label when called as severity_badge(severity); (8) test_aria_label_with_prefix — severity_badge('ok', 'Verdict severity') produces aria-label="Verdict severity: ok".

Add severity_badge(severity, aria_label=None) macro to badges.html. Color map: ok->bg-green-600, minor->bg-yellow-600, moderate->bg-orange-600, serious->bg-red-600. Fallback: bg-gray-600. Same <span> structure and conditional aria-label pattern as status_badge.

Write failing unit tests for indicator_dot macro in test_badge_macros.py. TestIndicatorDot class: (1) test_connected_green — connected=true produces bg-green-500; (2) test_disconnected_red — connected=false produces bg-red-500; (3) test_connected_aria_label — indicator_dot(true, 'Graphiti') produces aria-label="Graphiti connected"; (4) test_disconnected_aria_label — indicator_dot(false, 'Mem0') produces aria-label="Mem0 disconnected"; (5) test_role_status — output contains role="status"; (6) test_dot_classes — output contains 'w-3 h-3 rounded-full'; (7) test_renders_div — output uses <div> not <span>.

Add indicator_dot(connected, label) macro to badges.html. Renders
.

Write failing structural tests in test_badge_macros.py verifying templates import macros after refactoring. TestTemplateMacroImports class reads template files directly via pathlib. (1) test_tasks_imports_status_badge — tasks.html contains "from 'macros/badges.html' import status_badge"; (2) test_orchestrators_imports_status_badge — orchestrators.html contains same import; (3) test_recon_imports_badges — recon.html contains import of both status_badge and severity_badge; (4) test_memory_imports_indicator_dot — memory.html contains import of indicator_dot. All 4 fail before refactoring.

Refactor tasks.html: add {% from 'macros/badges.html' import status_badge %} at top. Replace 12-line if/elif/else badge block (lines 20-31) with {{ status_badge(task.status) }}. No aria_label passed — tasks.html badges currently lack aria-labels. Run test_orchestrator_panel.py (tasks.html is {% include %}'d by orchestrators.html) to verify all existing tests pass. Step-7 test_tasks_imports_status_badge now passes.

Refactor orchestrators.html: add {% from 'macros/badges.html' import status_badge %} at top. Replace running/completed if/else block (lines 12-16) with {{ status_badge('running' if orch.running else 'completed', aria_label='Orchestrator status', color='bg-green-600' if orch.running else 'bg-gray-600') }}. Color override preserves existing green=running, gray=completed behavior. Progress bar stays inline. Run test_orchestrator_panel.py to verify all tests pass including TestOrchestratorBadgeAriaLabels. Step-7 test_orchestrators_imports_status_badge now passes.

Refactor recon.html: add {% from 'macros/badges.html' import status_badge, severity_badge %} at top. (1) Burst state (lines 37-41): replace if/else with {{ status_badge(agent.state, aria_label='Burst state') }}. (2) Verdict severity (lines 81-91): replace 10-line if/elif chain with {{ severity_badge(verdict.severity, aria_label='Verdict severity') }}. (3) Run status (lines 124-136): replace 12-line if/elif chain with {{ status_badge(run.status, aria_label='Run status') }}. Run test_recon_panel.py to verify all tests pass including TestReconBadgeAriaLabels (11 tests). Step-7 test_recon_imports_badges now passes.

Refactor memory.html: add {% from 'macros/badges.html' import indicator_dot %} at top. (1) Graphiti dot (line 13): replace inline div with {{ indicator_dot(status.graphiti.connected|default(false), 'Graphiti') }}. (2) Mem0 dot (line 23): replace with {{ indicator_dot(status.mem0.connected|default(false), 'Mem0') }}. (3) Taskmaster dot (line 33): replace with {{ indicator_dot(status.taskmaster is defined and status.taskmaster.connected|default(false), 'Taskmaster') }}. Leave Write Queue 3-state dot inline. Run test_memory_partial.py to verify all tests pass including TestMemoryDotAriaLabels (11 tests). Step-7 test_memory_imports_indicator_dot now passes.

design_decisions.decision: status_badge and severity_badge use optional aria_label parameter (default None = no attribute)
design_decisions.rationale: tasks.html badges currently have no aria-labels while orchestrators.html and recon.html badges do. Making aria_label optional (None omits the attribute entirely) preserves exact current behavior for each template. This is a pure refactoring with zero behavioral changes.

design_decisions.decision: status_badge accepts optional color override parameter to resolve semantic conflicts
design_decisions.rationale: 'running' means 'alive/healthy' in orchestrators (green) but 'in-progress' in recon runs (blue). Rather than maintaining multiple color maps, a simple color override at the call site keeps the common case clean while handling the one collision explicitly: status_badge('running', color='bg-green-600' if orch.running else ...). Existing tests assert bg-green-600 for running orchestrators, so this preserves exact behavior.

design_decisions.decision: Single comprehensive color map in status_badge covering all badge domains
design_decisions.rationale: Burst states (bursting/idle), run statuses (rolled_back/circuit_breaker), orchestrator statuses, and task statuses all use identical badge HTML structure. A unified 13-entry map with gray fallback eliminates domain-specific badge macros. Only severity_badge warrants a separate macro because verdict severities have a distinct graduated color scale (ok/minor/moderate/serious).

design_decisions.decision: Leave Write Queue 3-state indicator dot inline in memory.html
design_decisions.rationale: Write Queue uses 3 states (dead->bg-red-500, pending->bg-yellow-500, healthy->bg-green-500) with 3 distinct aria-labels. This doesn't fit the binary indicator_dot(connected, label) signature. A specialized tri-state macro for a single use case would add complexity without reducing duplication.

design_decisions.decision: Leave progress bar segment colors in orchestrators.html as inline HTML
design_decisions.rationale: Progress bar segments are <div> elements with dynamic width-percentage styles, structurally different from badge <span> elements. They share color values with badges but have no duplicated HTML pattern to consolidate.

design_decisions.decision: Test macros in isolation via app's Jinja2 environment with env.from_string()
design_decisions.rationale: Reuses the same Jinja2 environment and FileSystemLoader as the app. Fast unit-level tests render macros directly without HTTP routes or mock data. The 42+ existing integration tests then guard against regressions during template refactoring (steps 8-11).

reuse.what: Existing integration tests as regression safety net (42+ tests across 3 files)
reuse.where: test_orchestrator_panel.py (TestOrchestratorBadgeAriaLabels 4 tests, TestTaskTable 2 badge tests, TestOrchestratorRouteBasics badge assertions), test_recon_panel.py (TestReconBadgeAriaLabels 11 tests, TestReconRoute badge color tests), test_memory_partial.py (TestMemoryPartialDots 7 tests, TestMemoryDotAriaLabels 11 tests)
reuse.how: These tests assert exact CSS class strings (bg-green-600, bg-red-500, etc.) and exact aria-label attributes in rendered HTML. After replacing inline badge logic with macro calls in steps 8-11, all must pass with zero modifications, proving the refactoring preserves identical output.

reuse.what: Jinja2Templates environment auto-resolves macros/ subdirectory path
reuse.how: Since macros/ is a subdirectory of the configured templates directory, {% from 'macros/badges.html' import ... %} resolves automatically. No app.py changes or additional template path configuration needed.

reuse.what: Shared badge HTML structure pattern across all templates
reuse.where: All 4 partial templates — 20 instances of identical span structure
reuse.how:
Every status/severity badge uses: {text}. Every indicator dot uses:
. Macros encapsulate these two structures.

reuse.what: conftest.py client fixture and mock data helpers
reuse.where: dashboard/tests/conftest.py (client fixture), test_orchestrator_panel.py (_patch_orchestrator_data), test_recon_panel.py (_patch_recon_data, MOCK_* constants), test_memory_partial.py (_ONLINE_STATUS)
reuse.how: Existing integration tests and helpers remain unchanged. New macro unit tests use direct Jinja2 rendering (no route mocking needed).
